summary: 10 items surfaced from 26 qualifying stories; full Firebase crawls completed where reachable.

items.name: DeepSeek V4 Preview
items.summary: Preview MoE model with stronger agent and reasoning capabilities.
items.why_included: The discussion focused on adoption, pricing, and coding workflows around the release.
items.evidence: Commenters said DeepSeek 'just does what I ask,' discussed subsidized pricing, and referenced Cecli as an open-source coding assistant built to work well with DeepSeek.

items.name: Cecli
items.summary: Open-source AI CLI coding assistant tuned for DeepSeek.
items.why_included: It was mentioned positively as a useful companion tool in the DeepSeek thread.
items.evidence: A commenter said they are involved in Cecli, described it as designed to work well with DeepSeek, and called it efficient for agentic programming on a budget.

items.name: Pollen
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Pollen – distributed WASM runtime, no control plane, single binary
items.summary: Distributed WASM runtime in a single binary, with no control plane.
items.why_included: Commenters called it impressive and discussed using it in place of Tailscale.
items.evidence: A commenter called it incredible, another compared it to an AWS-style service catalog, and the maker said they use it in place of Tailscale for homelab applications.

items.name: DAC
items.source_story_title: Show HN: DAC – open-source dashboard as code tool for agents and humans
items.summary: Open-source dashboard-as-code tool for agents and humans.
items.why_included: The discussion surfaced useful alternatives and concrete workflow tradeoffs for LLM-built dashboards.
items.evidence: Commenters asked about Vega-Lite as a go-to data-viz DSL, debated YAML versus JSX, and discussed using LLMs to write dashboards.

items.name: Vega-Lite
items.source_story_title: Show HN: DAC – open-source dashboard as code tool for agents and humans
items.summary: Declarative data-viz DSL for charts and dashboards.
items.why_included: It was singled out as a favorite way to generate visualizations with Claude.
items.evidence: A commenter called it their go-to data viz DSL with Claude, directly recommending it in the thread.

items.name: Dotcl
items.why_included: Commenters reacted like it was a useful new toy and a plausible weekend project.
items.evidence: People called it a new toy in their toolbox, said the timing was great, and pointed out MonoGame integration plus possible Unity or Godot use.

items.name: Databasus
items.source_story_title: Barman – Backup and Recovery Manager for PostgreSQL
items.summary: Tiny web interface for scheduling PostgreSQL backups.
items.why_included: It stood out as a practical self-hosting utility in the Barman discussion.
items.evidence: A commenter called it a remarkably simple backup scheduler, said they use it in a homelab, and reported that it works great.

items.name: Ti-84 Evo
items.summary: TI's new graphing calculator aimed at education and nostalgia.
items.why_included: The thread drew enthusiastic hardware nostalgia and practical calculator talk.
items.evidence: Commenters praised the interface, discussed RPN and calculator-app alternatives, and compared it with models like DM42n and HP 50G emulators.

items.name: NetHack 5.0.0
items.summary: NetHack's new release with updated client and gameplay polish.
items.why_included: HN users were excited enough to recommend companion clients and revisit the game.
items.evidence: Commenters highly recommended the 3D client, described long-haul play on laptops, and called NetHack one of the best open source projects.

items.name: NetHack 3D client
items.summary: 3D NetHack client with a web demo and GitHub repo.
items.why_included: It was explicitly recommended as a way to play the new NetHack release.
items.evidence: A commenter said they highly recommend the 3D client because it works almost everywhere and hoped it would be updated for 5.0.0 soon.
